title: Planning Collapse And Recovery Survey
description: A short research survey about what happens when plans break under deadline pressure.
confirmationMessage: Thank you. This helps decide whether this problem is urgent enough to build for, or whether the work should stay research-first.

analysis.primary_validation_rule: Pain is provisionally validated if at least one qualified segment shows recurring plan collapse, material cost, current-tool recovery failure, and willingness to try a recovery tool during a real pressure period.
analysis.qualified_respondent_filter: student, founder/builder, engineer, freelancer, researcher, or knowledge worker who reports at least occasional deadline/exam/work-crunch pressure.

segment_validation_thresholds.collapse_recency_positive: 60%+ report serious plan collapse within the last month
segment_validation_thresholds.collapse_frequency_positive: 50%+ report collapse at least a few times per month during pressure periods
segment_validation_thresholds.recovery_delay_positive: 40%+ take at least 15 minutes to know what to do next or give up on the plan
segment_validation_thresholds.pressure_trial_positive: 40%+ would try immediately or if setup was under 2 minutes

scale_direction_notes.current_tools_fail: Reverse-coded for pain: 1 means current tools mostly fail, 5 means current tools help recovery. Treat 1 or 2 as the current-tool-failure pain signal.
scale_direction_notes.cost_severity: Higher is more costly: treat 4 or 5 as material pain.
scale_direction_notes.overrun_cascade: Higher is more cascading damage: treat 4 or 5 as cascade pain.

runtime_fit_notes.overload_entry_behavior: Secondary runtime-fit signal, not primary pain validation. Messy-note dumping supports quick-capture fit. Avoid-looking responses are a separate high-value visibility/avoidance signal to inspect by segment.
runtime_fit_notes.post_collapse_action: Secondary runtime-fit signal for what Lyra would replace, compress, or augment after collapse.
runtime_fit_notes.timer_tolerance: Secondary instrumentation-friction signal; do not treat timer willingness as proof of pain without the pain cluster.

analysis.pivot_signal: If no qualified segment shows the pain cluster and runtime-fit signals, pause broad product push and narrow discovery to a more specific pressure context or pure research.

sections.title: Your Work Context
sections.description: Start here. This survey is about deadline pressure and plan-reality drift.

questions.title: Which best describes you right now?
How often do you have periods where deadlines, exams, projects, or obligations pile up?

options.label: A few times a semester / quarter

questions.title: When deadlines, tasks, or obligations pile up, what is your most natural first move?

options.label: Dump everything into a messy note/message
Open a task app / to-do list
Ask ChatGPT or another AI assistant what to do
Start working on the first urgent thing
Avoid looking at the full situation
I do not usually feel overloaded

questions.title: When was the last time your plan for a day or week seriously collapsed?

options.label: This does not really happen to me

sections.title: Plan Collapse
sections.description: Answer from a real recent example if you can.

questions.title: After the last time your plan collapsed, what did you actually do next?

options.label: Replanned manually in a calendar/task app
Asked ChatGPT or another AI assistant for help
Froze / avoided deciding what to do next
Gave up on the plan for the day

questions.title: What was the first sign the plan was going off-track?

options.label: One task took much longer than expected
I avoided or delayed the hardest task
An interruption/context switch broke the plan
Too many deadlines became visible at once
I was too tired/stressed to execute the plan

questions.title: When a plan starts going wrong, do you usually see it coming?

options.label: I usually see it coming but cannot stop it
It usually catches me off guard
I only realize after I am already behind
My plans do not usually go wrong

questions.title: When one task overruns, how often does it damage the rest of your plan?
How costly is a plan collapse when it happens?
What has plan collapse cost you before? Select all that apply.
During a normal pressure period, how often does this kind of collapse happen?

sections.title: Current Recovery Tools
sections.description: This section tests whether existing tools already solve the pain.

questions.title: What do you currently use to manage overloaded days or weeks?

options.label: I keep it in my head

questions.title: When the plan breaks, how well do your current tools help you recover?
When your plan breaks, what do your current tools fail to show clearly? Select all that apply.

options.label: What to do after I fall behind
How long the work really took
Why I stopped, paused, or switched
Whether something is finished, partly done, or abandoned
Which deadline or obligation is now at risk

questions.title: After your plan breaks, how long does it usually take to know what to do next?

options.label: I usually give up on the plan for the day
This does not apply to me

positive_values: I usually give up on the plan for the day

sections.title: Would A Recovery Tool Matter?
sections.description: No product pitch: answer based on whether this would be worth friction during a real pressure period.

questions.title: Which day-1 output would be most useful before an app knows your personal patterns?

options.label: A pressure map of visible work vs available time
A recovery inbox for missed/overdue planned work
A warning that today's plan is unrealistic
Help choosing what to resume, drop, or postpone
A summary of open loops from yesterday
One short question to resolve stale or paused work

questions.title: Would you start/stop timers for serious work if the main benefit was better recovery after overruns, pauses, or missed blocks?

options.label: Yes, but only during exams/deadlines/crunch weeks
Maybe, if starting/stopping took one tap

questions.title: During a real high-pressure week, would you try a tool that helps convert broken plans into next actions?

options.label: Yes, I would try it immediately
Probably, if setup was under 2 minutes
Maybe, but only if someone I trust recommended it

positive_values: Yes, I would try it immediately
Probably, if setup was under 2 minutes

questions.title: If a tool reliably helped during crunch weeks and then disappeared, how disappointed would you be?

options.label: I do not think I would use it

questions.title: What would make you stop using a tool like this after 3 days?
Optional: leave an email/Telegram/Reddit handle if you are open to a 10-minute follow-up.

sections.title: Quick Exit
sections.description: This branch is for people who do not experience the core pain.

questions.title: Why does this not feel like a problem for you?
What does your current system look like when things get busy?
Is there any context where this would become painful for you?
